Only 3/4 human signals present (author+LinkedIn=false, first-party data=false, original visual=true, 3+ citations=true). High AI-content-flag risk
FAQPage schema has 7 Questions but page shows ~13 FAQ pairs
No first-party data signals ("we tested", "our data shows", "we analyzed"). Add at least one concrete observation
Intro earns attention 3/10 — Opens with duplicated H1, a bureaucratic TL;DR, and a meta description before the human hook appears.

patch.target: TL;DR: A Q1 2026 health check is a 15-minute wellness audit that measures your progress across physical, mental, and lifestyle goals using Dubai's regional health guidelines and WHO frameworks.
patch.before: TL;DR: A Q1 2026 health check is a 15-minute wellness audit that measures your progress across physical, mental, and lifestyle goals using Dubai's regional health guidelines and WHO frameworks.
patch.after: Most Dubai New Year resolutions are dead by Ramadan. I'm a GP here, and March — not January — is when I rebuild mine.
patch.rationale: Humanization axis "Intro earns attention" scored 3/10: Opens with duplicated H1, a bureaucratic TL;DR, and a meta description before the human hook appears.

instructions.evidence: Person missing recommended: jobTitle, sameAs, hasCredential
Entity of type FAQPage has no @id — cross-page interconnection blocked
Primary keyword "q1 health check" not in first 8 words of TL;DR
Author has no jobTitle or description — credentials not stated
Specific human voice vs committee 5/10 — Voice oscillates between a believable GP persona and generic SEO listicle boilerplate.

patch.target: DHA-aligned screenings and reviews are essential for UAE health optimisation.
patch.before: DHA-aligned screenings and reviews are essential for UAE health optimisation.
patch.after: If you skip the DHA screenings, you're guessing — and I've watched too many patients guess their way into a diagnosis.
patch.rationale: Humanization axis "Specific human voice vs committee" scored 5/10: Voice oscillates between a believable GP persona and generic SEO listicle boilerplate.

instructions.evidence: Genuine opinions vs safe claims 6/10 — A few sharp opinions (ignoring WHO, Ramadan killing resolutions) sit beside many hedged platitudes.

patch.target: Quarterly reviews work well for chronic conditions, nutrition, and fitness tracking.
patch.before: Quarterly reviews work well for chronic conditions, nutrition, and fitness tracking.
patch.after: Quarterly reviews are overkill for fitness tracking but genuinely change outcomes for chronic conditions — don't waste them on step counts.
patch.rationale: Humanization axis "Genuine opinions vs safe claims" scored 6/10: A few sharp opinions (ignoring WHO, Ramadan killing resolutions) sit beside many hedged platitudes.

instructions.evidence: Unexpected phrasings 6/10 — '45°C car park' and 'January self had no idea Ramadan was coming' are vivid; most other phrasing is stock.

patch.target: Checking in each March with a DHA-aligned, 15-minute Q1 health check helps Dubai and Abu Dhabi residents stay grounded, realistic, and proactive about their 2026 wellness goals.
patch.before: Checking in each March with a DHA-aligned, 15-minute Q1 health check helps Dubai and Abu Dhabi residents stay grounded, realistic, and proactive about their 2026 wellness goals.
patch.after: A March check-in won't transform you — it just stops July-you from being furious at January-you.
patch.rationale: Humanization axis "Unexpected phrasings" scored 6/10: '45°C car park' and 'January self had no idea Ramadan was coming' are vivid; most other phrasing is stock.

instructions.evidence: Point of view vs both-sides survey 6/10 — Has a POV (anti-WHO, pro-March review) but dilutes it with neutral 'friendly note' filler.

patch.target: Friendly note: This is not about perfection, but about small, useful adjustments for the next quarter.
patch.before: Friendly note: This is not about perfection, but about small, useful adjustments for the next quarter.
patch.after: Perfection isn't the goal; I'd rather you hit 60% of a realistic plan than 100% of a fantasy one.
patch.rationale: Humanization axis "Point of view vs both-sides survey" scored 6/10: Has a POV (anti-WHO, pro-March review) but dilutes it with neutral 'friendly note' filler.

instructions.evidence: specificity 6/10 — Several bullet points and transition sentences float in abstractions ('small, useful adjustments', 'longer-lasting health benefits', 'sustainable progress'). The one genuinely specific data point — 'A typical DHA-aligned panel at Valeo runs AED 650 and covers HbA1c, LDL, vitamin D, and TSH — expect results in 48 hours' — is excellent and shows what the rest of the post lacks. The 'Key takeaways' and 'Long-term behaviours' sections in particular rely on vague language where named numbers, clinic names, or cited figures would anchor the claims.

patch.target: Quarterly reviews work well for chronic conditions, nutrition, and fitness tracking.
patch.before: Quarterly reviews work well for chronic conditions, nutrition, and fitness tracking.
patch.after: Quarterly reviews cut HbA1c drift in type-2 patients by ~0.4% in one BMJ cohort, and in Dubai's context they catch the Ramadan-to-summer fitness gap before it becomes a six-month backslide.
patch.rationale: Quality axis "specificity" scored 6/10: Several bullet points and transition sentences float in abstractions ('small, useful adjustments', 'longer-lasting health benefits', 'sustainable progress'). The one genuinely specific data point — 'A typical DHA-aligned panel at Valeo runs AED 650 and covers HbA1c, LDL, vitamin D, and TSH — expect results in 48 hours' — is excellent and shows what the rest of the post lacks. The 'Key takeaways' and 'Long-term behaviours' sections in particular rely on vague language where named numbers, clinic names, or cited figures would anchor the claims.

No TL;DR block (expected

or paragraph starting with 'TL;DR:')

Author has no jobTitle or description — credentials not stated
No first-party data signals ("we tested", "our data shows", "we analyzed"). Add at least one concrete observation

Specific human voice vs committee 5/10 — Voice oscillates between a believable GP persona and generic SEO listicle boilerplate.
Genuine opinions vs safe claims 6/10 — A few sharp opinions (ignoring WHO, Ramadan killing resolutions) sit beside many hedged platitudes.
Unexpected phrasings 6/10 — '45°C car park' and 'January self had no idea Ramadan was coming' are vivid; most other phrasing is stock.
Specific citations (names, places, prices, dates) 5/10 — Mentions DHA, MOHAP, Ramadan, 1.5-2L water, but no prices, clinic names, specific dates, or people.
Point of view vs neutral survey 3/10 — Presents consensus guidance without taking a clear position on what actually works.

intro_hook 4/10 — The opening sentence is pure throat-clearing: it restates the title verbatim and adds no tension, surprise, or specificity that earns continued reading. There is no hook, no stakes, no counterintuitive claim. The reader has no reason to feel this post is for them rather than a generic wellness article.

specificity 6/10 — Several bullet points and transition sentences float in abstractions ('small, useful adjustments', 'longer-lasting health benefits', 'sustainable progress'). The one genuinely specific data point — 'A typical DHA-aligned panel at Valeo runs AED 650 and covers HbA1c, LDL, vitamin D, and TSH — expect results in 48 hours' — is excellent and shows what the rest of the post lacks. The 'Key takeaways' and 'Long-term behaviours' sections in particular rely on vague language where named numbers, clinic names, or cited figures would anchor the claims.
